title: IMD issues heatwave warning as Mumbai sizzles under relentless March sun - Mid-day
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Maharashtra
full_text:
Youngsters beat the heat in the Arabian Sea off the coast at Girgaon on Monday. PIC/ATUL KAMBLE
Mumbai and neighbouring districts will likely witness scorching conditions with the IMD issuing a yellow alert for the city, Thane, and Raigad, indicating heatwave conditions in isolated pockets on Monday and Tuesday, and for Palghar and Ratnagiri on Monday. The IMD had issued heatwave warnings for March 8 and 9. On March 10, Mumbai recorded the highest March temperature in five years - 40 degrees C.
Pedestrians brave the scorching heat near Churchgate station on Monday. Pic/Ashish Raje
On Monday, Santacruz recorded a temperature of 36.5 degrees C, with IMD indicating temperatures could shoot up to 38 degrees on Tuesday. While Coastal Maharashtra is likely to experience hot and dry weather, the IMD issued a rainfall alert for the state's interior districts, indicating thundershowers, lightning, and gusty winds up to 40 to 50 kmph in Satara, Sangli, Solapur, Latur, and Dharashiv.
Mumbai and Thane (Monday and Tuesday)
Palghar and Raigad (Monday)
Do
>> Drink sufficient water, as often as possible, even if not thirsty
>> Consume oral rehydration solution, homemade drinks like lassi, torani (rice water), lemon water, and buttermilk
>> Wear lightweight, light-coloured, loose cotton clothes
>> If you feel dizzy or nauseous, see a doctor immediately
>> Employers must ensure that labourers do not work outside between noon and 3 pm
A pigeon cools off in a puddle on Veera Desai Road in Andheri West. PIC/SATEJ SHINDE
Don't
>> Avoid going out between 12 noon and 3 pm
>> Do not go out barefoot or without a face and head cover
>> Avoid cooking during peak hours. Open doors and windows to ventilate cooking area adequately
>> Avoid alcohol, tea, coffee, and carbonated soft drinks, which dehydrate the body
>> Avoid high-protein, spicy, and oily food. Do not eat stale food
(According to National Disaster Management Authority)
